data.title: Warren calls on Federal Reserve nominee to explain Epstein links
data.description:
Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-Mass.), the top Democrat on the Senate Banking Committee, asked Federal Reserve Chair nominee Kevin Warsh to clarify his links to Jeffrey Epstein.

In a Wednesday letter to Warsh, Warren referenced two emails released by the Department of Justice (DOJ) in which the nominee is listed as a guest to a pair of gatherings that Epstein seemingly attended. 

One, in September 2010, lists the guests for a dinner party hosted by former British politician William Astor. Warsh is listed as a guest alongside his wife, Jane Lauder. Dozens of others are listed, including Epstein and his longtime accomplice, Ghislaine Maxwell. President Trump and First Lady Melania Trump, Donald Trump Jr. and his then-wife Vanessa Trump and Jared Kushner and Ivanka Trump were also listed. 

Another, in December of that year, lists Lauder and Warsh as among a large group of people traveling to the Caribbean island of St. Barthélemy for Christmas. Trump is not listed as part of that group, while Maxwell is.  

Warren wrote to Warsh that “it is essential that Congress and the public fully understand the extent of any interactions or relationship you had with” Epstein. She also noted that the events that Warsh attended occurred after the disgraced financier pleaded guilty to soliciting a minor for prostitution and soliciting a prostitute in 2008, and after he had settled multiple civil lawsuits with accusers. 

The Massachusetts Democrat asked Warsh to clarify whether he traveled to St. Barthélemy in December 2010 or January 2011, whether he attended a gathering in either of those months where Epstein was present and if so, the extent of their interactions, if any. 

She also asked whether he attended an event that Epstein or Maxwell was present at in September, October or November 2010, and whether the president or any members of his family were in attendance. 

Lastly, Warren asked for details on any additional communications Warsh had with Epstein or Maxwell not referenced in the emails. 

Trump in January nominated Warsh to lead the central bank, succeeding Jerome Powell, whose term ends in May. But even before her letter on Wednesday, Warren expressed concern over the former Fed board of governors member, slamming him for previously supporting high interest rates before, in her view, pivoting in order to get the nomination from the president. 

Trump criticized Powell throughout last year for not backing a larger or earlier interest rate cut.

“Kevin Warsh is not a guy who will make an independent economic decision. It tells me Kevin Warsh is a sock puppet,” she argued on CNBC’s “Squawk Box” last month.

Meanwhile, Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.), a member of the Banking Committee, has vowed to hold up Warsh’s nomination until the DOJ’s probe of Powell concludes.

data.title: House passes bill that would deport noncitizens for harming police animals
data.description:
The House on Thursday passed a bill that would make foreign nationals and noncitizens inadmissible to the U.S. and subject to deportation if they are convicted of, or admit to, harming animals used in law enforcement.

The legislation, dubbed the “Bill to Outlaw Wounding of Official Working Animals” (BOWOW) Act passed the lower chamber by a vote of 228-190. Fifteen Democrats voted with Republicans and Rep. Kevin Kiley (I-Calif) in favor of the measure. 

The Democrats who supported the bill were Reps. Henry Cuellar (Texas), Don Davis (N.C.), Sharice Davids (Kan.), John Garamendi (Calif.), Jared Golden (Maine), Vicente Gonzalez (Texas), Josh Gottheimer (N.J.), Greg Landsman (Ohio), Marie Gluesenkamp Perez (Wash.), Chrissy Houlahan (Pa.), Adam Gray (Calif.), Josh Riley (N.Y.), Eric Sorensen (Ill.), Tom Suozzi (N.Y.) and Eugene Vindman (Va.).

The bill now heads to the Senate, where it will need some Democratic support to overcome the 60-vote filibuster rule.

Rep. Ken Calvert (R-Calif.), who introduced the bill, said in a statement back in July that “the dogs and horses on the front lines of our federal law enforcement efforts alongside our officers deserve our protection.” 

“The BOWOW Act sends a clear message that we will stand up for our four-legged friends and have zero tolerance for any immigrants who assault them,” he said at the time.

The California Republican had introduced the bill after an Egyptian man pleaded guilty to federal charges in June for kicking a U.S. Customs and Border Protection agriculture detector named Freddie, a five-year-old beagle.

But some Democratic opponents of the bill have argued that it would do little to strengthen existing federal law.

“It’s against the law now to kick a police dog…but the opportunity to introduce a redundant bill was apparently impossible for us politicians to pass up,” Rep. Jamie Raskin (D-Md.), ranking member of the House Judiciary Committee, said during a mark-up hearing in November.

“I’m unaware of any mad rush of immigrants roaming the country and attacking police animals,” Raskin added. “So, I’m not sure what crisis this surplus language is responding to.”

Others argued the bill is hypocritical, pointing to Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem’s past admission that she shot her own dog.

Trump fired Noem from her role earlier this month and tapped Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.) to replace her.

“R’s want increased immigration punishment for animal cruelty. But their bill notably excludes the animal cruelty of the Trump official responsible for overseeing the immigration system — @Sec_Noem — who shot and killed her own puppy,” Rep. Dan Goldman (D-N.Y.) wrote on the social media platform X in November.

data.title: Jeffries says Democrats will oppose rule governing spy powers bill
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said Thursday that Democrats are lining up en masse against helping Republicans advance legislation to a final vote to extend the government’s warrantless surveillance powers.

Jeffries acknowledged that the vote on the bill itself will divide Democrats, and he declined to say how he will vote himself. 

“But what you can expect is that every single Democrat will oppose the rule,” he told reporters in the Capitol. “And then we’ll see where we go from there.”

GOP leaders are scrambling to reauthorize Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), which allows federal intelligence officials to gather communications from foreigners living abroad. The provision is highly controversial because the process inevitably captures communications from Americans, and there are lawmakers on both sides of the aisle vowing to oppose the bill without reforms. 

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) has so far ruled out amendments to the law, hoping instead to push a “clean” reauthorization. 

There are enough lawmakers in both parties to pass the legislation, but it has to reach the floor first. 

Under normal circumstances, that process would require the passage of a rule governing debate on the underlying bill. But already Rep. Lauren Boebert (R-Colo.) is vowing to oppose the rule, unless the Senate passes voting reforms demanded by President Trump. And several other Republicans have said they might join her. 

Because of the Republicans’ narrow House advantage — and the tradition that the minority party tends to oppose all rules put forth by the majority — just two GOP defections could be enough to sink the rule and prevent a vote on the underlying FISA bill.

In rare cases, Democrats have crossed the aisle to help GOP leaders pass rules governing legislation that the Democrats support. Jeffries, though, said this is not one of those occasions.

data.title: Mace hits Mullin over Paul spat, calls selection as DHS secretary 'unreal'
data.description:
Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.) went after Sen. Markwayne Mullin (R-Okla.) over his tension with Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.), which was notably on display during a hearing Wednesday on his possible new role as Department of Homeland Security (DHS) secretary.

“There’s a difference between right and wrong. Every one knows this is wrong and few have spoken up,” Mace said in a post on the social platform X on Thursday. 

“Rand Paul had to have part of his lung removed after he was assaulted. Unreal we would promote someone who advertises violence as the answer when they have a political disagreement,” she added.

Mace’s post featured a clip from the hearing posted by Paul, in which he pressed Mullin about calling him a “snake” and saying he understood why Paul’s neighbor attacked him.

“You told the media that I was a ‘freaking snake’ and that you completely understood why I had been assaulted. I was shocked that you would justify and celebrate this violent assault that caused me so much pain and my family so much pain,” Paul said in his opening remarks.

“I just wonder if someone who applauds violence against their political opponents is the right person to lead an agency that has struggled to accept limits to the proper use of force.”

“You have never had the courage to look me in the eye and tell me that the assault was justified, so today you’ll have your chance,” Paul said.

DHS has been mired in controversy in recent months, especially after the fatal shootings of Alex Pretti and Renee Good in Minneapolis in January. Democrats have demanded changes to immigration enforcement and have kept DHS shut down for over a month by being unwilling to compromise on legislation that does not include immigration reforms.

The department’s secretary, Kristi Noem, was booted from the job a few weeks ago to serve in a different position, with Mullin as her replacement.

On Thursday, Mullin’s nomination to lead DHS advanced out of the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee.

The Hill has reached out to Mullin’s office for comment.

data.title: Trump ribs Japanese PM on Pearl Harbor in Oval Office talk on Iran
data.description:
President Trump ribbed the nation of Japan over its World War II bombing of Pearl Harbor in a Thursday Oval Office meeting with Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi.

Taking questions from reporters, Trump said he would call on a “beautiful person from Japan.”

Asked why he didn’t tell allies like Japan about the conflict in Iran ahead of time, Trump invoked the Dec. 7, 1941 bombing in Hawaii. 

“You don’t want to signal too much… We didn’t tell anybody about it because we wanted surprise,” he responded. 

“Who knows better about surprise than Japan? Why didn’t you tell me about Pearl Harbor?” Trump added jokingly to Takaichi.

The 1941 incident resulted in the U.S. entry into World War II.

The prime minister and the president were set to discuss trade and energy costs, among other issues, as the Iran conflict has led to soaring oil prices worldwide.

Takaichi said in her own remarks that the global economy is set to take a “huge hit” from the war.

data.title: Comer on Democrats walking out of Epstein meeting: 'Low point' of investigation
data.description:
House Oversight Committee Chair James Comer (R-Ky.) on Thursday called the moment when Democrats walked out of a closed-door meeting with Attorney General Pam Bondi a “low point” in the committee’s investigation into convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.

“The way the Democrats behaved was clearly a premeditated stunt to go out with their fake outrage,” Comer told reporters. “They said Pam Bondi wasn’t answering their questions. They didn’t ask any questions. And look, I was embarrassed by the Democrats.”

He added that some of his GOP allies on the committee have also “sadly” said and done things “that have been [embarrassing].”

“But I tell you what, you know, you just have to face the fact that we have a lot of dead weight on this committee, and the behavior of the Democrat yesterday was unacceptable,” The Kentucky Republican continued, adding later that he “wasn’t expecting” Democrats to walk out of the meeting.

Comer accused Democrats of “secretly tape-recording” the meeting based on subsequent reporting, calling it “a violation of the rules.”

“But I don’t care, I stand by what I said,” he added. “It’s unfortunate that the Democrats –– they displayed a gross lack of seriousness that’s very concerning moving forward.”

House Oversight Democrats “all just f‑‑‑ing had it and walked out” after Comer “decided to use that opportunity to say that [Rep. Summer Lee (D-Pa.)] was ‘b‑‑‑‑ing,'” Rep. Yassamin Ansari (D-Ariz.) said in a video posted to social platform X.

Ansari noted that she and her colleagues pressed Bondi on whether she would testify under oath after she was subpoenaed in the probe. The attorney general refused to answer the question. It was after Lee asked Comer if he would take action should Bondi not come forward that eventually led Democrats to walk out.

Rep. Robert Garcia (D-Calif.), the committee’s ranking member, called it a “fake hearing” and “complete disrespect of the process.”

“[Bondi] was not under oath and she would not commit to actually going under oath and following the law,” Garcia said, flanked by other Oversight Democrats including Ansari, in a separate video shared on X. “This has been completely set up in a way that’s been irresponsible and quite frankly, we believe, a way for the attorney general to get out of her answering questions under oath and to the public, which we will not accept.”

Rep. Ayanna Pressley (D-Mass.), who also sits on the committee, called on Bondi to testify under oath, accusing Republicans of holding the closed-door meeting “filled with lies.”

“The American people, and most importantly survivors, are through with this White House cover-up,” she wrote on X.

data.title: In the strait of Hormuz, where are the allies?
data.description:
On 12 March, Mojtaba Khamenei made his first public statement as supreme leader. After a quick nod to his father, he directed that “the lever of blocking the Strait of Hormuz must definitely continue to be used.” The fight to keep the Strait of Hormuz open has become the Iranian main effort. 

Over the past few days, world oil prices are spiking, and gas pumps are too. In the U.S., regular gas is nearly $3.83 per gallon. Last month, it averaged $2.90. Europe is seeing even higher prices, as it remains far more dependent on Persian Gulf oil than the energy-independent U.S. In London, gas is $6.89; in Paris, $7.87; and in Frankfurt, $8.93. One can hope the U.S. Navy is close to executing its long-anticipated mine-countermine operations plans. But as it looks over the waters of the strait, it must be asking the question: “Where are the allies?” 

Maritime coalitions are hardly new. In 2008, sea traffic in the Indian Ocean was nearly paralyzed by Somali pirates hijacking ships, taking hostages, and demanding outrageous ransoms. A frustrated Condoleezza Rice, then secretary of State, built a coalition including China, Russia, Britain, France, and a host of other countries within months. Together, they created the Contact Group on Piracy off the Coast of Somalia, focused on a comprehensive approach to the root causes of the problems, to include military and operational coordination, legal issues, self-defense and diplomatic efforts.

The effort also obtained crucial support from Kenya to prosecute captured pirates rather than continue the previous “catch-and-release” practice. The operation also obtained UN Security Council Resolution 1851 in December 2008 authorizing nations to use “all necessary means” to suppress piracy not just at sea, but also on land and in the airspace of Somalia. It was the first time the permanent members of the UN Security Council would deploy forces on the same side.

Combined Task Force 151, along with EU Operation Atalanta and NATO Operation Allied Protector, made a difference. The greatest number of hijackings occurred in 2011, with 237 attacks and 28 successful hijackings. Only 15 attacks and two successful hijackings occurred in 2013 and in 2025 there were only five attacks and three successful hijackings. In short, the international community recognized it had a problem, and an international coalition solved it.

Two reasons clearly stand out in hampering the formation of a coalition to maintain an open Strait of Hormuz. The first is chronic underspending on defense budgets, which has left limited resources focused on more immediate needs. Personnel costs, operations, and the acquisition of advanced technology have meant that critical mine countermeasures capabilities, especially ships equipped with modern systems, have fallen near the bottom of defense priorities.

The U.S. Navy has four remaining Avenger-class mine countermeasures ships of 14 built, all forward deployed in Sasebo, Japan. It also fitted four Landing Class Ships (LCS) with mine countermeasure modules to work alongside25 Sea Dragon mine-hunting helicopters. At least three LCS are deployed to the Middle East.

Allied help is needed to augment the U.S. capabilities. Countermine fleets maintained by allied nations could significantly expand the U.S. capability if brought together. Alone, France has as many as 16, Germany 12 and Great Britain at least seven.

The second concern is the diplomatic reluctance for military support from the countries that possess the capabilities, even with the greatest stake in the outcome. Yet, while these nations may not wish to assist the United States and Israel in the air war, it is clearly in their interest to help secure the sea lanes and prevent the Strait of Hormuz from closing. Additionally, they can work with the United States to reassure shipping companies and encourage international cargo carriers to continue operating through the region.

The Tanker War of 1981 to 1988 offers a model for such operations.

During the Iran-Iraq War, Iraq was the first to attack Iranian ships, with 283 missions throughout the war, hoping to choke off the Iranian economy by cutting off oil exports. Iran followed suit in 1984 with 168 attacks overall. In response, a collection of the U.S., United Kingdom, France, Italy, the Netherlands and Belgium sailed more than 75 Western warships and specialized minesweepers, to include most of the 14 U.S. Navy Avenger–class mine countermeasures ships. The Soviet Union also maintained a small squadron of three to four ships to protect Soviet-chartered tankers.

Of note, the operation was a collection of nations working independently rather than a coalition of nations under unified command and control. For those nations seeking distance from the U.S., this provides another alternative for the nations affected by Iranian efforts to close the Strait. Additionally, Iran may be reluctant to fire on European warships from countries currently expressing neutrality in the war.

The new supreme leader has thrown down the gauntlet. There is a critical need to respond to Iranian attacks on international shipping but also an opportunity for a coalition, or collection, of nations to come together in service of their national and shared interests. Whatever the disagreements surrounding the conduct of the current conflict, such a fleet is essential. One can only hope that the State Department is actively working on diplomacy and that Central Command planners, along with allied military liaison teams assigned to the U.S. headquarters, are already preparing the mission. 

Retired U.S. Army Brigadier General Mark Kimmitt served as Assistant Secretary of State for Political-Military Affairs from August 2008 to January 2009.

data.title: 6 governor races shift to Democrats: Election handicapper
data.description:
A leading election handicapper on Thursday shifted its ratings of six gubernatorial races toward Democrats, though Republicans are well positioned to hold onto their advantage in the country’s governorships this fall. 

Sabato’s Crystal Ball moved Georgia’s open governor’s seat from “leans Republican” to the “toss-up” column, while Arizona moved out of “toss-up” territory to “leans Democratic.” 

Ohio’s open gubernatorial seat also shifted from “likely Republican” to “leans Republican,” and three “likely Democratic” seats in Minnesota, New York and Rhode Island moved under the “safe” blue label.

“Despite a litany of gubernatorial rating changes today in favor of Democrats, Republicans may still break the usual pattern of midterm net gubernatorial losses for the president’s party,” Sabato’s Kyle Kondik and J. Miles Coleman wrote in an analysis. 

“Meanwhile, Democrats have an opportunity of their own to do something they haven’t done since the 2010 election: hold a majority of the nation’s governorships. But it’s a challenging path.” 

Republicans hold 26 governorships, to Democrats’ 24 seats. Under Sabato’s current ratings, which project one Democrat-held seat in Kansas will flip, Democrats would have to defend all their other seats and flip three Republican-held slots to win control of a majority of governorships across the country.

The open seat in Georgia, vacated by term-limited Gov. Brian Kemp (R), is “the clearest Democratic pickup opportunity.” In Arizona, Gov. Katie Hobbs (D) is one of just two Democratic incumbents seeking reelection in states that President Trump won in 2024. 

And in Ohio, recent polling has signaled that Democrat Amy Acton is leading 2024 Republican presidential candidate Vivek Ramaswamy in the red state, offering a potential pickup in the race to replace term-limited Gov. Mike DeWine (R).

data.title: Jeffries says Dems will soon push another resolution to end war in Iran
data.description:
House Democrats will soon launch another effort to end President Trump’s war with Iran, House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) announced on Thursday.

“What we’re focused on now is stopping this reckless war of choice,” Jeffries told reporters in the Capitol. 

Democrats have already brought one war powers resolution to the floor this month. Sponsored by Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.), that legislation would have required the administration to cease its military operations against Tehran unless the campaign is explicitly approved by Congress. 

The measure failed on March 5 by a narrow vote of 212 to 219. All but two Republicans opposed it, while four Democrats crossed the aisle to defeat it.

Jeffries said the new resolution would contain substantial differences from the first, to reflect the developments in the conflict over the last three weeks as well as the sentiment of voters, a majority of whom oppose the war, according to recent polls. 

“It will be different in nature to some degree because we’re now approximately three weeks into the war, and we’ve got a lot more information that can be presented to the American people as it relates to the concerns that they, of course, have had from the very beginning,” the New York Democrat noted.

He declined to provide further details. 

Jeffries also predicted that the measure would gain more Democratic support than the Massie-Khanna bill, which was opposed by Reps. Jared Golden (D-Maine), Juan Vargas (D-Calif.) Greg Landsman (D-Ohio) and Henry Cuellar (D-Texas). 

He did not say which of the four defectors might change their vote on the next resolution. 

It’s also unclear how many Republicans could reverse course since the first resolution hit the floor. Massie and Rep. Warren Davidson (R-Ohio) were the only two GOP lawmakers to support the initial measure, but conditions have changed since then, as gas prices have spiked and the administration is now asking for $200 billion to fund the conflict.

Even if it fails, much of the Democrats’ strategy is to force a public debate on Iran on the House floor, where they will trumpet their case that Tehran posed no imminent threat and the administration has no cohesive plan for getting out of the conflict. 

“There’s no plan, no strategy, no objective,” Jeffries said Thursday. “They clearly didn’t anticipate what was going to happen in the Strait of Hormuz. Republicans clearly did not anticipate the fact that more than a dozen countries would be drawn into this conflict in the Middle East.”

“Regime change hasn’t taken place, which was one of the stated goals,” he continued. “And now the administration apparently is threatening to send ground troops into Iran, which we know will be a disaster.” 

The Democratic leader did not specify when the measure would come to the floor, other than to say it would happen “in short order.”

data.title: Japan, Europeans signal 'readiness to contribute' to efforts to keep Strait of Hormuz open
data.description:
The leaders of Japan and several European nations condemned recent Iranian attacks in the Gulf states, which have effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz, a major oil trading channel. 

“We express our readiness to contribute to appropriate efforts to ensure safe passage through the Strait,” the leaders wrote in a Thursday letter. “We welcome the commitment of nations who are engaging in preparatory planning.”

Japan, the United Kingdom, France, Germany, Italy and the Netherlands all signed on to the joint statement, conveying “deep concern” about the Iranian strikes’ impact on the global energy supply. 

The price of Brent crude oil spiked to $119 per barrel on Thursday before dropping back down after the Iranian military hit a gas facility in the United Arab Emirates. The country also targeted energy fields in Qatar and Saudi Arabia. 

The International Energy Agency (IAE) said that its members will release 400 million barrels of oil from their reserves to offset rising global energy prices. A total of 32 countries, including the U.S., are IAE members. President Trump authorized the release of 172 million barrels from the U.S.’s Strategic Petroleum Reserve last week.

The six co-signers of Thursday’s letter commended the IAE’s decision and committed to taking additional steps toward stabilizing the global energy markets. 

“We will also work to provide support for the most affected nations, including through the United Nations and the IFIs,” they wrote.

“Maritime security and freedom of navigation benefit all countries,” the leaders continued. “We call on all states to respect international law and uphold the fundamental principles of international prosperity and security.”

Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, one of the letter’s co-signers, is in Washington, D.C., for a meeting with Trump today. The two leaders are expected to discuss shared trade interests and the ongoing military operations in Iran.
